questions.question: What does an INNER JOIN return?
options: All rows from both tables regardless of matches
Only rows where there is a match in both tables
All rows from the left table only
All rows from the right table only
questions.answer: Only rows where there is a match in both tables

questions.question: What does a LEFT JOIN return?
options: Only rows that match in both tables
All rows from the right table and matching rows from the left
All rows from the left table and matching rows from the right
The Cartesian product of both tables
questions.answer: All rows from the left table and matching rows from the right

questions.question: In a LEFT JOIN, what appears in the right-side columns when there is no match?
options: An empty string
Zero
NULL
The default value of the column
questions.answer: NULL

questions.question: What does a CROSS JOIN produce?
options: Only matching rows between two tables
The Cartesian product of all rows from both tables
A union of both tables
Rows where the primary keys match
questions.answer: The Cartesian product of all rows from both tables

questions.question: If table A has 5 rows and table B has 4 rows, how many rows does a CROSS JOIN return?
What is a self-join?
options: A join that creates a copy of the table
A join where a table is joined to itself
A join that automatically matches primary keys
A join between two tables with the same schema
questions.answer: A join where a table is joined to itself

questions.question: Why are table aliases useful in JOIN queries?
options: They improve query execution speed
They make queries shorter and more readable
They are required by SQL syntax for all joins
They automatically index the joined columns
questions.answer: They make queries shorter and more readable

questions.question: Which keyword can be omitted when defining a table alias?
options: JOIN
ON
AS
FROM
questions.answer: AS

questions.question: Which SQL clause specifies the condition for matching rows in a JOIN?
options: WHERE
HAVING
ON
USING
questions.answer: ON

questions.question: Which type of JOIN does SQLite NOT support?
options: INNER JOIN
LEFT JOIN
RIGHT JOIN
CROSS JOIN
questions.answer: RIGHT JOIN

questions.question: How can you simulate a RIGHT JOIN in SQLite?
options: Use a CROSS JOIN with a WHERE clause
Swap the table order and use a LEFT JOIN
Use a FULL OUTER JOIN instead
It cannot be simulated in SQLite
questions.answer: Swap the table order and use a LEFT JOIN

questions.question: What does a FULL OUTER JOIN return?
options: Only rows that match in both tables
All rows from both tables, with NULLs where there is no match on either side
The Cartesian product of both tables
All rows from the left table only
questions.answer: All rows from both tables, with NULLs where there is no match on either side

questions.question: Given: SELECT s.name, e.grade FROM students s INNER JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id; Which students appear in the results?
options: All students in the students table
Only students who have at least one enrollment
Only students with a grade of A
Only the first student in the table
questions.answer: Only students who have at least one enrollment

questions.question: In a self-join to find pairs, why is the condition a.id < b.id used?
options: To sort results by ID
To ensure only rows with small IDs are included
To avoid duplicate pairs and self-pairs
To limit the number of results
questions.answer: To avoid duplicate pairs and self-pairs

questions.question: How do you join three tables together in a single query?
options: Use nested SELECT statements only
Chain multiple JOIN clauses one after another
Use UNION to combine three separate queries
Create a temporary table first
questions.answer: Chain multiple JOIN clauses one after another

questions.question: Which query finds students not enrolled in any course using a LEFT JOIN?
options: SELECT * FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id WHERE e.student_id = 0;
SELECT * FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id WHERE e.student_id IS NULL;
SELECT * FROM students s INNER JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id WHERE e.grade IS NULL;
SELECT * FROM students s CROSS JOIN enrollments e WHERE e.student_id IS NULL;
questions.answer: SELECT * FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id WHERE e.student_id IS NULL;

questions.question: What is the purpose of the ON clause in a JOIN?
options: To specify which columns to display
To define the condition that relates rows between the joined tables
To filter the final result set
To order the output rows
questions.answer: To define the condition that relates rows between the joined tables

questions.question: Which query correctly joins students, enrollments, and courses?
options: SELECT * FROM students s, enrollments e, courses c;
SELECT * FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id;
SELECT * FROM students s JOIN enrollments e JOIN courses c;
SELECT * FROM students s CROSS JOIN enrollments e CROSS JOIN courses c ON s.id = e.student_id;
questions.answer: SELECT * FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id JOIN courses c ON c.id = e.course_id;

questions.question: What does GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT e.grade) do in a JOIN query?
options: Counts the number of distinct grades
Returns the highest grade
Concatenates all unique grade values into a comma-separated string
Groups rows by grade value
questions.answer: Concatenates all unique grade values into a comma-separated string

questions.question: Which JOIN type is most commonly used to find matching records between two related tables?
options: CROSS JOIN
LEFT JOIN
INNER JOIN
FULL OUTER JOIN
questions.answer: INNER JOIN

questions.question: In a LEFT JOIN, which table is considered the 'left' table?
options: The table listed after the JOIN keyword
The table listed in the FROM clause before the JOIN keyword
The table with fewer rows
The table with the primary key
questions.answer: The table listed in the FROM clause before the JOIN keyword

questions.question: What happens if you omit the ON clause in an INNER JOIN?
options: It returns an empty result set
It behaves like a CROSS JOIN producing a Cartesian product
It produces a syntax error
It automatically joins on matching column names
questions.answer: It produces a syntax error

questions.question: Which clause is used with GROUP BY to filter groups after aggregation in a JOIN query?
options: WHERE
HAVING
ON
FILTER
questions.answer: HAVING

questions.question: To count students enrolled per course using a LEFT JOIN, which aggregate function and grouping should you use?
options: SUM(e.student_id) with GROUP BY c.name
COUNT(e.student_id) with GROUP BY c.id
MAX(e.student_id) with GROUP BY c.name
AVG(e.student_id) with GROUP BY c.department
questions.answer: COUNT(e.student_id) with GROUP BY c.id

questions.question: What is the key difference between WHERE and ON in a JOIN query?
options: There is no difference; they are interchangeable
ON specifies the join condition between tables, while WHERE filters the combined result set
WHERE is used for joins and ON is used for filtering
ON only works with INNER JOIN, while WHERE works with all joins
questions.answer: ON specifies the join condition between tables, while WHERE filters the combined result set
